name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَا جَعَلْنَا لِبَشَرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ٱلْخُلْدَ ۖ أَفَإِي۟ن مِّتَّ فَهُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
We did not give immortality to any human before you. If you are fated to die, will they live on forever? (34)
```

```arabic
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَنَبْلُوكُم بِٱلشَّرِّ وَٱلْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً ۖ وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of test, and to Us you will be brought back. (35)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا رَءَاكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَذْكُرُ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
Whenever the faithless see you they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who remembers your gods?’ while they remain defiant towards the remembrance of the All-beneficent. (36)
```

```arabic
خُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ (٣٧)
```

```english
Man is a creature of haste. Soon I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten. (37)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٨)
```

```english
And they say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (38)
```

```arabic
لَوْ يَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ حِينَ لَا يَكُفُّونَ عَن وُجُوهِهِمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا عَن ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
If only the faithless knew about the time when they will not be able to keep the Fire off their faces and their backs, nor will they be helped! (39)
```

```arabic
بَلْ تَأْتِيهِم بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
Indeed, it will overtake them suddenly, dumbfounding them. So they will neither be able to avert it, nor will they be granted any respite. (40)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
Apostles were certainly derided before you; but those who ridiculed them were besieged by what they had been deriding. (41)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَن يَكْلَؤُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۗ بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Who can guard you, day and night, against [the punishment of] the All-beneficent [should He want to punish you]?’ Indeed, they are disregardful of their Lord’s remembrance. (42)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُم مِّن دُونِنَا ۚ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
Do they have gods besides Us to defend them? Neither can they help themselves, nor can they shield [the idolaters] from Us. (43)
```

```arabic
بَلْ مَتَّعْنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ طَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۗ أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَآ ۚ أَفَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
Indeed, We have provided for them and their fathers until they lived on for long years. Do they not see how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Are they the ones who will prevail? (44)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُنذِرُكُم بِٱلْوَحْىِ ۚ وَلَا يَسْمَعُ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا مَا يُنذَرُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘I indeed warn you by the means of revelation.’ But the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned. (45)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِن مَّسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
Should a whiff of your Lord’s punishment touch them, they will surely say, ‘Woe to us! We have indeed been wrongdoers!’ (46)
```

```arabic
وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَـٰسِبِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
We shall set up just scales on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least. Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed We shall produce it and We suffice as reckoners. (47)
```

## (Commentary)

As a continuation of the discourse regarding prophethood (النبوة), this section mentions some of what the polytheists said concerning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as their saying: "He will die, and then we shall be rid of him and find rest," and their saying in mockery of him: **أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ** — "Is this the one who mentions your gods?" (21:36). It also mentions their saying in mockery of the Resurrection (البعث) and the Day of Judgment (القيامة) with which they were warned: **مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" (21:38). Included herein is the response to their claims, a warning and threat directed at them, and consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ أَفَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخالِدُونَ** — **We did not grant immortality to any human before you. If you die, are they then to be immortal?** (21:34). It appears from the verse that they used to console themselves with the idea that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) would eventually die, thereby freeing them from his call and saving their gods from his criticism, as has been narrated about them in verses such as: **نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ** — **"We await for him some calamity of fate"** (52:30). Thus, He responded to this by stating that We have not granted immortality (الخلد) to any human being before you such that this could be expected for you; rather, you shall die and they too shall die. Your death will not benefit them in the least, for they will not attain immortality by your death. Everyone is mortal. Furthermore, their short, term-limited lives are not free from trial (الفتنة) and divine testing (الامتحان الإلهي), for no human is exempt from this in the life of this world. Nor are they beyond Our dominion in the Hereafter; rather, to Us they shall return, and We shall hold them to account and recompense them for what they have done.

His saying: **أَفَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخالِدُونَ** — **"If you die, are they then to be immortal?"** He did not simply say "they are immortal." The interrogative here is for denial (الإنكار), serving to negate the restriction of the heart (قصر القلب). It is as if it were said: Their saying, "We await for him some calamity of fate," is the speech of one who sees for himself an immortality in which you are a competitor; as if were you to die, he would walk away with immortality, seize it, and live a perpetual, pleasant, and luxurious life. But it is not so; rather, every soul shall taste death, and the life of this world is built upon trial and testing. There is no meaning to a permanent trial or an eternal test; rather, they must return to their Lord so He may recompense them for that through which He tested and distinguished them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً وَإِلَيْنا تُرْجَعُونَ** — **Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial, and to Us you will be brought back.** (21:35). The term **soul (النفس)** — based on what reflection upon its instances of usage yields — has a primary meaning that is identical to the meaning of that to which it is annexed (المضاف إليه). Thus, the "soul of a thing" means the thing itself; the "soul of a human" means the human himself; and the "soul of a stone" means the stone itself. If it were severed from annexation, it would have no realized meaning. Based on this meaning, it is used for verbal emphasis (التأكيد اللفظي), as when we say: "Zayd himself (نفسه) came to me," or to convey its essence, as when we say: "The self (نفس) of Zayd came to me."

In this sense, it is applied to everything, even to Him, the Exalted, as He said: **كَتَبَ عَلى نَفْسِهِ الرَّحْمَةَ** — **"He has made mercy incumbent upon Himself"** (6:12), and He said: **وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ اللهُ نَفْسَهُ** — **"Allah warns you of Himself"** (3:28), and He said: **تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ** — **"You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self"** (5:116).

Subsequently, the usage of this term became common specifically for the human person (شخص الإنسان), which is the entity composed of spirit (روح) and body (بدن). Thus, it acquired a meaning in itself even if severed from annexation. The Exalted said: **هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها** — **"It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate"** (7:189), meaning from a single human person. And He said: **مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّما قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً وَمَنْ أَحْياها فَكَأَنَّما أَحْيَا النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً** — **"whoever slays a soul, without [its being guilty of] slaying a soul or [of] corruption on the earth, it is as though he had slain all mankind; and whoever saves a life, it is as though he had saved all mankind"** (5:32), meaning whoever kills a human and whoever gives life to a human. Both meanings are combined in His saying: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ تُجادِلُ عَنْ نَفْسِها** — **"every soul will come pleading for itself"** (16:111); the first "soul" carries the second meaning (the person), and the second "soul" carries the first meaning (the self).

Then, they used it to refer to the human spirit (الروح الإنساني) because life, knowledge, and power — which constitute the human being — subsist through it. From this is the saying of the Exalted: **أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ** — **"Give up your souls! Today you shall be requited with a humiliating punishment"** (6:93).

These two applications — namely the second and third — were not consistently applied to things other than humans, such as plants and other animals, except according to technical scientific terminology. In common usage (عرفا), a single plant or animal is not called a "soul" (نفس), nor is the governing principle of its body called a "soul." Yes, blood is sometimes called "soul" (نفس) because life depends upon it, and from this comes the term "flowing blood" (النفس السائلة).

Similarly, in the language, the term "soul" is not applied in the second or third sense to angels or jinn, even if it is believed that they possess life. The usage of "soul" for them does not appear in the Qur'an either, even though the verses state that the jinn have obligations (تكليف) like humans, as well as death and resurrection (حشر). He said: **وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** — **"I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me"** (51:56), and He said: **فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ** — **"among the nations of jinn and humans who passed away before them"** (46:18), and He said: **وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعاً يا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ قَدِ اسْتَكْثَرْتُمْ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ** — **"On the day He will muster them all together: ‘O company of jinn, you have certainly [seduced] many of the humans’"** (6:128). This is what is gathered regarding the meaning of "soul" according to linguistic custom.

As for death (الموت), it is the loss of life and its effects, such as consciousness (الشعور) and will (الإرادة), from that which is naturally disposed to be characterized by them. The Exalted said: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ** — **"you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will cause you to die"** (2:28), and He said regarding idols: **أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ** — **"They are dead, not living"** (16:21). As for it being the separation of the soul from the body by the severance of its governing attachment (تعلقها التدبيري), as defined in rational inquiries, or it being the transition from one abode to another, as in the Prophetic tradition, these are meanings revealed by reason (العقل) or tradition (النقل) beyond what is established in common usage. It is well known that death, in the sense mentioned, only characterizes the human being composed of spirit and body with respect to his body; for it is the body that is characterized by the loss of life after possessing it. As for the spirit, nothing has appeared in the speech of the Exalted that speaks of it being characterized by death, just as this has not appeared regarding the angels. As for His saying: **كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ** — **"Everything is to perish except His Face"** (28:88), and His saying: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **"The Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon"** (39:68), it will be explained, God willing, that perishing (الهلاك) and swooning (الصعق) are distinct from death, even if they sometimes coincide with it.

Thus, it has become clear from what we have presented: First, that the intended meaning of "soul" in His saying: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ** — **"Every soul shall taste death"** is the human being — which is the second of its three usages — rather than the human spirit, since the attribution of death to the spirit is not established in the speech of the Exalted such that it could be interpreted as such.

Second: That the verse encompasses the human being specifically and not others like angels, jinn, and other animals, even if some of them are characterized by death, such as jinn and animals. A contextual clue (القرينة) for the verse's exclusivity to humans is His preceding saying: **وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ** — **"We did not grant immortality to any human before you"** and His subsequent saying: **وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً** — **"and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial"**, as we shall clarify.

A group of commentators mentioned that the intended meaning of "soul" in the verse is the spirit, but you have seen its contrary. Many of them insisted on the verse's generality for every living thing, including humans, angels, jinn, and other animals, and even plants if they possess true life; you have seen the flaws in this.

Among the most peculiar things said in establishing the verse's generality is what Imam al-Razi mentioned in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir*. After establishing that the verse is general for every possessor of a soul, he stated that the verse is qualified (مخصصة), for the Exalted has a "Self" (نفس) as He said, narrating from Jesus (peace be upon him): **تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ** — **"You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self"**, despite death being impossible for Him, the Glorified. Likewise, inanimate objects have "souls" (نفوس) yet they do not die. He then said: "A qualified general term (العام المخصوص) remains a proof, so it continues to be applied according to its literal meaning (ظاهره) regarding everything except what was excluded. This invalidates the view of the philosophers regarding human spirits, detached intellects (العقول المفارقة), and celestial souls (النفوس الفلكية) that they do not die." End quote.

In this, there are several points: First, the "soul" in the sense applied to the Exalted and to everything is the "soul" in the first of the three usages we presented, which is only used when annexed, as in the verse he cited as evidence. However, the "soul" in the verse under discussion is severed from annexation, so it is certainly not intended in that sense. Thus, "soul" remains in one of the other two meanings, and you have seen that the third meaning is also not intended, so the second remains.

Second: His denial of death for inanimate objects contradicts the saying of the Exalted: **كُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ** — **"you were lifeless and He gave you life"** and His saying: **أَمْواتٌ غَيْرُ أَحْياءٍ** — **"They are dead, not living"**, and other such verses.

Third: His claim that the verse's generality invalidates the philosophers' view regarding human spirits, detached intellects, and celestial souls is an error. These are rational issues (مسائل عقلية) sought through the path of demonstration (البرهان). A demonstration is a proof that yields certainty (اليقين). If the arguments they established for them are all or some of them demonstrations, as they claim, then no literal appearance (الظهور) from the verse can be established against them, for literal appearance is a probabilistic proof (حجة ظنية). How can the coexistence of knowledge (العلم) with a probabilistic opinion (الظن) to the contrary be conceived? And if they are not demonstrations, then the issues are not proven, and there is no need for a probabilistic opinion to the contrary.

Furthermore, His saying: **كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ** — **"Every soul shall taste death"**, just as it is a confirmation and establishment of the content of His preceding saying: **وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ** — **"We did not grant immortality to any human before you"** etc., it is also a preamble and preparation for His subsequent saying: **وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً** — **"and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial"** — meaning: We test you with what you dislike, such as illness and poverty, and what you desire, such as health and wealth, as a trial. It is as if it were said: We give each of you a limited, term-bound life and We test you therein with evil and good as a trial, then to your Lord you shall return, and He will judge for you and against you.

In this is an indication of the reason for the necessity of death for every living soul: that the life of every soul is a life of trial and tribulation (ابتلاء). It is well known that a trial is a preliminary matter (أمر مقدمي), and it is necessary that a preliminary does not last forever without reaching an end. It is also necessary that behind every preliminary there is an objective (ذو مقدمة), and after every trial there is a station where its result is determined. Thus, for every living soul, there is an inevitable death, followed by a return to Allah, the Glorified, for the final judgment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ وَهُمْ بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ** — **When the faithless see you, they only take you in derision: ‘Is this the one who mentions your gods?’ while they are faithless at the mention of the All-beneficent.** (21:36). The particle **إِنْ** is negatory, and the meaning of His saying: **إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً** is the restriction (قصر) of their treatment of him to taking him as a mockery; that is, they do not treat you except as a mockery to be ridiculed.

His saying: **أَهذَا الَّذِي يَذْكُرُ آلِهَتَكُمْ** — **"Is this the one who mentions your gods?"** — the implied meaning being "they say" or "saying: Is this the one..." — is a narration of their words of mockery. The mockery lies in referring to him with this description. Their intention was his mentioning their gods with evil, but they did not state it explicitly out of "etiquette" toward their gods. This is similar to His saying: **قالُوا سَمِعْنا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقالُ لَهُ إِبْراهِيمُ** — **"They said, ‘We heard a youth mentioning them, who is called Abraham’"** (21:60).

His saying: **وَهُمْ بِذِكْرِ الرَّحْمنِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ** — **"while they are faithless at the mention of the All-beneficent"** is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال) from the pronoun in "they take you," or from the subject of the implied "they say," which is closer. The gist is that they are indignant on behalf of their gods against you when you say of them that they neither benefit nor harm — which is a word of truth — so they confront you only with mockery and insult; yet they are not indignant on behalf of Allah when His mention is disbelieved, and they themselves are the disbelievers.

The intended meaning of "the mention of the All-beneficent" (ذكر الرحمن) is mentioning Him, the Exalted, as the Bestower of every mercy and the Giver of every blessing, the necessary consequence of which is that He, the Exalted, is the Lord Whom it is obligatory to worship. It has also been said that the intended meaning of "the mention" is the Qur'an.

The meaning is: When those who disbelieve — namely the polytheists — see you, they do not take you nor deal with you except with mockery and ridicule, saying to one another: "Is this the one who mentions your gods?" — meaning with evil. Thus, they are indignant for their gods when they are mentioned, while they are disbelievers in the mention of the All-beneficent, not considering it a crime nor being indignant for it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ سَأُرِيكُمْ آياتِي فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ** — **Man is a creature of haste. I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten.** (21:37). The polytheists, in addition to their disbelief in the prophetic call, used to mock the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) whenever they saw him, which was an increase in disbelief and rebellion. Mockery of something only occurs by assuming it is a jest (هزل) and not serious (جد), thus meeting jest with jest. However, He, the Exalted, took this mockery of theirs as something serious and not a jest. Thus, mockery after disbelief became an exposure to divine punishment after a prior exposure, which is the seeking of haste (استعجال) for the punishment. For they were not content with the signs that came to them while they were in a state of well-being, and they sought signs that would requite them for what they had done. Therefore, He, the Glorified, considered their mockery after disbelief as seeking haste in seeing the signs — namely the signs accompanied by punishment — and informed them that He would show them these signs.

Thus, His saying: **خُلِقَ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ** — **"Man is a creature of haste"** is a metaphor (كناية) for man's extreme haste, as if he were created from haste itself and knows nothing else. This is similar to saying: "So-and-so is all good" or "all evil," or "created from good" or "from evil." This is more eloquent than saying, "How hasty he is" or "How intense is his seeking of haste." The speech is delivered in a tone of amazement (التعجيب).

In this is a belittling of their affair, and that He does not hasten their punishment because they cannot escape Him.

His saying: **سأُرِيكُمْ آياتِي فَلا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ** — **"I will show you My signs. So do not ask Me to hasten."** The subsequent verse testifies that the intended meaning of showing the signs is their punishment with the fire of Hell, which is His saying: **لَوْ يَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا حِينَ** — **"If the faithless only knew when..."** etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — **They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’** (21:38). The speakers are those who disbelieve, and the addressees are the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers. The apparent requirement was for them to say: "if you [singular] are truthful," but they shifted to what you see in order to add to their attempt to frustrate the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) — by demanding from him what he is not capable of — the misleading of the believers and inciting them against him. The "promise" is what was contained in the previous verse and is explained by the following verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَوْ يَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا حِينَ لا يَكُفُّونَ عَنْ وُجُوهِهِمُ النَّارَ وَلا عَنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ** — **If the faithless only knew when they will not be able to keep off the Fire from their faces or their backs, nor will they be helped!** (21:39). The particle **لَوْ** is for wishing (التمني), and **حِينَ** is the object of "knew" according to what has been said. His saying: **لا يَكُفُّونَ عَنْ وُجُوهِهِمُ النَّارَ وَلا عَنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ** — **"they will not be able to keep off the Fire from their faces or their backs"** means they cannot repel it when it seizes them from their front and their back. In this is an indication of its encompassing them.

His saying: **وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ** — **"nor will they be helped"** is conjoined to what preceded it because its meaning returns to a negation by way of contrast. The meaning is: they cannot repel the Fire independently by themselves, nor by the help of anyone who might assist them in repelling it.

The verse is in the position of an answer to their question about the appointed time. The meaning is: If only those who disbelieve knew the time when they will not repel the Fire from their faces nor from their backs, neither independently by themselves nor being helped in repelling it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلْ تَأْتِيهِمْ بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّها وَلا هُمْ يُنْظَرُونَ** — **Indeed, it will come to them all of a sudden and confound them, and they will not be able to repel it, nor will they be granted any respite.** (21:40). What the context requires is that the subject of "it will come to them" is a pronoun referring to the Fire, rather than the Hour as some have suggested. The sentence is a transition (إضراب) from His saying in the previous verse: **لا يَكُفُّونَ** — **"they will not be able to keep off"** etc. It is not a transition from an implied statement before it — such as "the signs do not come to them according to their proposal, but they come to them suddenly" — nor from His saying: **لَوْ يَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** — **"If the faithless only knew"** — by claiming it carries the meaning of negation, i.e., "they do not know that, but it comes to them suddenly." All these are views that the context rejects.

The meaning of the Fire coming to them suddenly (بغتة) is that it surprises them from where they do not know from whence it comes and encompasses them. For this is a necessary consequence of what Allah described of its nature in His saying: **نارُ اللهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ** — **"It is the Fire of Allah, set ablaze, which overspreads the hearts"** (104:6-7), and His saying: **النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ** — **"the Fire whose fuel is humans"** (2:24), and His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ وَما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ** — **"Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell"** (21:98). The Fire that has this status seizes the interior of the human just as it seizes the exterior, equally. It is not like the fire of this world such that it approaches from one direction to another and seizes the exterior before the interior and the outside before the inside, such that it might grant them respite by crossing a distance, or by a gradual process in action, or a separation in direction, whereby one might scheme to repel it by shrinking away, or avoiding it, or presenting a barrier, or seeking refuge in a corner. Rather, it is with them just as their own selves are with them; it cannot be repelled since there is no difference in direction, nor does it accept respite since there is no distance between it and them. Thus, it allows them nothing upon its descent except bewilderment (البهت) and confusion.

So the meaning of the verse — and Allah knows best — is: they do not repel the Fire from their faces and backs; rather, it comes to them from where they do not perceive it nor know, thus being a sudden surprise to them, so they cannot repel it, nor are they granted respite in its arrival.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَحاقَ بِالَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** — **Apostles were certainly derided before you, then those who mocked them were beset by what they used to deride.** (21:41). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: The difference between **ridicule (السخرية)** and **derision (الهزء)** is that in ridicule there is the meaning of seeking humiliation (الذلة), for *taskhir* (تسخير) is subjugation. As for derision, it implies seeking to belittle the status (صغر القدر) through what appears in speech. End quote. **Besetting (الحيق)** is the descent or befalling. The intended meaning of "what they used to deride" is the punishment. In the verse is consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a frightening and threat for those who disbelieve.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ يَكْلَؤُكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ بَلْ هُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ** — **Say, ‘Who can guard you day and night from the All-beneficent?’ Indeed, they are regardless of the remembrance of their Lord.** (21:42). **Guarding (الكلاءة)** is protection. The meaning is: Ask them who protects them from the All-beneficent if He intends to punish them. Then He turned away from the effectiveness of exhortation and warning upon them, saying: **بَلْ هُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِمْ** — **"Indeed, they are regardless of the remembrance of their Lord"** — meaning the Qur'an — **مُعْرِضُونَ** — **"turning away"**, so they do not pay attention to it nor do they wish to listen to it when you recite it to them. It has also been said that "remembrance" (الذكر) refers to exhortations and proofs in general.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُمْ مِنْ دُونِنا لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَلا هُمْ مِنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ** — **Do they have any gods to defend them besides Us? They cannot help themselves, nor can they be protected from Us.** (21:43). The particle **أَمْ** is disjunctive (منقطعة), and the interrogative is for denial. Both "defend them" and "besides Us" are attributes of "gods." The meaning is: Rather, ask them, do they have gods besides Us who can defend them from Us?

His saying: **لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ** — **"They cannot help themselves"** etc., is a justification (تعليل) for the negation understood from the interrogative of denial; hence it was brought as a separate clause (الفصل). The implied meaning is: they do not have such gods because they cannot help themselves by helping one another, nor are they protected or guarded from Us. So how can they help their worshippers among the polytheists or grant them sanctuary? Some have mentioned that the plural pronouns refer to the polytheists, but the context rejects this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلْ مَتَّعْنا هؤُلاءِ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى طالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ** — **Indeed We provided for these and their fathers until their lives were long drawn out.** (21:44) to the end of the verse. This is a transition from the content of the previous verse, just as His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِمْ مُعْرِضُونَ** was a transition from what preceded it. The contents — as you see — are closely related.

His saying: **حَتَّى طالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ** — **"until their lives were long drawn out"** is the limit (غاية) for the duration of the provision (التمتع) indicated by the previous sentence. The implied meaning is: rather, We provided for these polytheists and their fathers, and the provision continued for them until their lives were long drawn out, so they were deceived by that, forgot the remembrance of Allah, and turned away from His worship. Such was the society of Quraysh; for after their father Ishmael, they were inhabitants of a secure sanctuary, enjoying various types of blessings brought to them, until they gained mastery over Mecca and expelled the tribe of Jurhum from it. Then they forgot the religion of their father Abraham which they were upon and worshipped idols.

His saying: **أَفَلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها** — **"Do they not see how We visit the land, reducing it at its edges?"** It is more appropriate to the context that the intended meaning of reducing the land at its edges is the extinction of some of the nations that inhabit it; for every nation has a term — no nation can precede its term, nor can they delay it. It has already been pointed out that the intended meaning of the "long life" granted to them is the long life of their society.

The meaning is: Do they not see that the land is being reduced of one nation after another through extinction by the command of Allah? So what prevents Him from destroying them? Are they then the victors if Allah, the Glorified, intends harm, destruction, or extinction for them?

Some discussion regarding this verse has already passed in its counterpart in Surah al-Ra‘d, so refer to it. Know that in these verses there are various instances of grammatical shift (الالتفات) which we have not addressed due to their clarity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنَّما أُنْذِرُكُمْ بِالْوَحْيِ وَلا يَسْمَعُ الصُّمُّ الدُّعاءَ إِذا ما يُنْذَرُونَ** — **Say, ‘I warn you only by the revelation.’ But the deaf do not hear the call when they are warned.** (21:45). That is, that which I warn you with is a divine revelation (وحي إلهي) in which there is no doubt. It only fails to produce its effect in you — which is guidance — because there is deafness in you such that you do not hear the warning. Thus, the deficiency is on your side, not in the revelation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلئِنْ مَسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ** — **If a breath of your Lord’s punishment were to touch them, they would surely say, ‘Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers.’** (21:46). A **breath (النفحة)** is a single occurrence of punishment. The meaning is that warning with the verses of the Remembrance (الذكر) does not benefit them; rather, these people require a breath of punishment until they are compelled, and then they will believe and confess their wrongdoing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً** — **We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least.** (21:47). **Justice (القسط)** is equity (العدل). It is an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for "the scales" or an attribute of "the scales" with an implied annexed word, the meaning being "the scales possessing justice." The discussion regarding the meaning of the Scale (الميزان) set up on the Day of Resurrection has already passed in the commentary on Surah al-A‘raf.

His saying: **وَإِنْ كانَ مِثْقالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنا بِها** — **"Even if it be the weight of a mustard seed, We shall produce it"**. The pronoun in "even if it be" refers to the weighed deed (العمل الموزون) indicated by the mention of the scales. That is, even if the weighed deed were the weight of a mustard seed in its heaviness, We would produce it, and sufficient are We as reckoners. The mustard seed is used as a proverb for its fineness, smallness, and insignificance. In this is an indication that the weighing (الوزن) is part of the reckoning (الحساب).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj who said: "When Gabriel announced to the Prophet his own death, he said: 'O Lord, who then is for my community?' So there was revealed: **وَما جَعَلْنا لِبَشَرٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ الْخُلْدَ** — **'We did not grant immortality to any human before you'** (21:34)."

**I say:** The context of the verses, which is a context of reproach (عتاب), does not harmonize with what was mentioned. Furthermore, this question does not suit the station of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Moreover, the announcement of his death was at the end of the Prophet's life, while the Surah is among the earliest Meccan Surahs.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Suddi who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) passed by Abu Sufyan and Abu Jahl while they were talking. When Abu Jahl saw him, he laughed and said to Abu Sufyan: 'This is the prophet of the sons of Abd Manaf.' Abu Sufyan became angry and said: 'What do you deny, that the sons of Abd Manaf should have a prophet?' The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) heard it, so he returned to Abu Jahl, confronted him, and frightened him, saying: 'I do not see you stopping until there befalls you what befell your uncle.' And he said to Abu Sufyan: 'As for you, you only said what you said out of tribal zeal (حمية).' Then this verse was revealed: **وَإِذا رَآكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً** — **'When the faithless see you, they only take you in derision'** (21:36)."

**I say:** This is like the previous one in the lack of a precise correspondence between the story and the verse.

In *al-Majma‘*, it is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "That the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) fell ill, so his brothers visited him and said: 'How do you find yourself, O Commander of the Faithful?' He said: 'In evil (بشر).' They said: 'This is not the speech of one like you.' He said: 'Indeed Allah, the Exalted, says: **وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً** — **"and We will test you with evil and good by way of trial"** (21:35). Thus, good (الخير) is health and wealth, and evil (الشر) is illness and poverty.'"

In the same source: Regarding His saying: **أَفَلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها** — **"Do they not see how We visit the land, reducing it at its edges?"** (21:44). It was said: "By the death of the scholars (العلماء)." And that was narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Its reduction is the passing away of its scholar."

**I say:** Some discussion regarding the meaning of the hadith has already passed in the commentary on Surah al-A‘raf.

In *al-Tawhid*, from ‘Ali (peace be upon him): In a tradition — where a man asked him about verses that were ambiguous to him — "As for the saying of the Blessed and Exalted: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً** — **'We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least'** (21:47), it is the scale of justice (ميزان العدل); the creatures will be taken by it on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, will judge the creation, some by means of others, with the scales."

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain of transmission to Hisham, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَنَضَعُ الْمَوازِينَ الْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ فَلا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْئاً** — **'We shall set up the scales of justice on the Day of Resurrection, and no soul will be wronged in the least'** (21:47). He said: 'They are the Prophets and the Successors (الأوصياء).'"

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Kafi* with a chain that is elevated (فيه رفع) to him (peace be upon him). We have cited other narrations with these meanings in the commentary on Surah al-A‘raf and discussed them as much as was possible.
